answer:
In Urdu poetry, *naseeb* (نصیب, from Arabic *naṣīb*, "portion" or "share") connotes not just inevitable destiny but a divinely apportioned lot—one's personal allotment from God, often with an intimate, distributive quality that invites personification as an active distributor or taker.[^1] English *fate*, by contrast, feels more impersonal and mechanistic, losing this relational sense of measured dispensation (like dividing an inheritance).[^2]

Consider this couplet from Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's *Diwan-e-Zauq*, where *naseeb* actively extracts revenge from the lover amid love's separations:

> Shab-e-vasl ke roz-e-firaq mein kya kya  
> Naseeb mujh se mere inteqaam lete hain  
> (*Transliteration*)[^3]  
> 
> For one brief night of union, my fates exact their shame;  
> Through endless days of parting, my revenge they claim, is what they take.  
> (*Translation*)[^4]

Here, *naseeb* is the grammatical subject wielding agency (*lete hain*, "they take") against the speaker. English *fate* can approximate this personification ("fate exacts revenge"), but it strains slightly against *fate*'s typical passivity; *naseeb*'s root as "share" more naturally evokes an entitled claimant seizing its due.

[^1]: Usage across the corpus (e.g., Zauq ch.8 [p-269606](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/08-balaaein-aankhon-se-un-ki-madaam-lete-hain/transliteration/#p-269606/); Iqbal *Bang-e-Dara Pt.2* ch.21 [p-d17409](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/transliteration/#p-d17409/)) consistently treats it as a bestowed or extracted portion, not mere inevitability.
[^2]: No work directly glosses the etymology (the corpus lacks lexicons), but poetic deployment implies this nuance versus *fate*'s cooler abstraction.
[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch.8 "Balaaein aankhon se un ki madaam lete hain" [p-e12715](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/08-balaaein-aankhon-se-un-ki-madaam-lete-hain/transliteration/#p-e12715/).
[^4]: *Ibid.*, [p-866af5](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/08-balaaein-aankhon-se-un-ki-madaam-lete-hain/translation/#p-866af5/).
